steps.prompt: Which is the main finite verb of the first clause?

hints.advesperascit: 'Advesperascit' is a finite verb but belongs to the causal clause after 'quoniam'
hints.inclinata est: 'Inclinata est' is a compound verb in the second causal clause

hints.to come early: 'Mane' as a noun means 'morning', but as a verb form it is from 'manere' (to remain)

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 2nd sing. would be 'manes' — 'Mane' is imperative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive 2nd sing. would be 'maneas' — 'Mane' is imperative
hints.noun (morning): 'Mane' can be a noun meaning 'morning', but here it is a verb form (imperative of 'manere')

hints.mano, manare, manavi, manatum: These are the parts of 'mano' (to flow/drip)
hints.moneo, monere, monui, monitum: These are the parts of 'moneo' (to warn/advise)
hints.mando, mandare, mandavi, mandatum: These are the parts of 'mando' (to command)

steps.reveal: advesperascit = it is growing toward evening (3rd sing. pres., impersonal)

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'advesperascat' — 'advesperascit' is indicative

hints.vesper, vesperi, —, —: 'Vesper' is a noun meaning 'evening' — 'advesperascit' is a verb (inchoative)
hints.vespero, vesperare, vesperavi, —: 'Vesperare' is not standard — 'advesperascit' is the impersonal inchoative form
hints.aspicio, aspicere, aspexi, aspectum: These are the parts of 'aspicio' (to look at) — unrelated to evening

steps.prompt: What kind of verb is 'advesperascit' (note the -sc- infix)?
steps.target: inchoative/inceptive verb (denoting the beginning of an action)
choices: inchoative/inceptive verb (denoting the beginning of an action)
steps.reveal: The -sc- infix marks an inchoative: 'it is beginning to become evening'

hints.frequentative verb: Frequentative verbs show repeated action (e.g., -itare) — -sc- marks inception
hints.desiderative verb: Desiderative verbs show desire (e.g., -urire) — -sc- marks beginning of action
hints.causative verb: Causative verbs cause an action — -sc- marks the onset/beginning of a state

steps.prompt: What words form the compound verb in the second clause?
steps.reveal: inclinata est dies = the day has declined

hints.inclinata only: 'Inclinata' alone is a participle — with 'est' it forms the perfect passive
hints.est only: 'Est' alone is the auxiliary — 'inclinata est' together is the perfect passive
hints.iam dies: 'Iam' is an adverb (already) and 'dies' is the subject — neither is a verb

hints.clino, clinare, clinavi, clinatum: 'Clino' in simple form is rare — 'inclinare' is the compound in- + clinare
hints.reclino, reclinare, reclinavi, reclinatu: These are the parts of 'reclino' (to lean back) — different prefix
hints.declino, declinare, declinavi, declinatu: These are the parts of 'declino' (to turn aside/decline) — different prefix

steps.reveal: dies = the day (nom., subject of 'inclinata est')

hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'diei' — 'dies' is nominative here

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'diei' ends in -ei
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'diei' ends in -ei
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'diei' ends in -ei

hints.dative plural of 'nos': 'Nobis' can be dative, but 'nobiscum' is the enclitic form of cum + ablative
hints.accusative plural: Accusative of 'nos' is 'nos' — 'nobiscum' is ablative with enclitic 'cum'

translation: Stay with us, because it is growing toward evening and the day has already declined
